You are working on beads issue kapow-c0s.1: Lefthook integration.

## WORKTREE SETUP — RUN FIRST
```bash
REPO_ROOT="$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)"
BRANCH_NAME="worktree/lefthook-integration"
WORKTREE_DIR="$REPO_ROOT/.claude/worktrees/$BRANCH_NAME"
if [ -d "$WORKTREE_DIR" ]; then echo "Worktree already exists, reusing"; else git worktree add "$WORKTREE_DIR" -b "$BRANCH_NAME"; fi
cd "$WORKTREE_DIR"
echo "CWD: $(pwd)"; echo "Branch: $(git branch --show-current)"
echo "$(pwd)" | grep -q '\.claude/worktrees/' && echo "WORKTREE VERIFIED" || echo "FATAL: NOT in a worktree"
```
ALL subsequent commands must run from this worktree directory.

## Issue Details
**kapow-c0s.1: Lefthook integration**

Integrate Lefthook so repo-local hook orchestration is fast, deterministic, and shared across humans and coding agents.

**AC**: Repo contains a documented Lefthook setup for the agreed local hooks. Hooks run reliably through repo-local commands rather than ad hoc per-user scripts. The setup is usable by humans and automation.

## Instructions
1. Read CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md for conventions and quality gate commands
2. Read the Makefile to understand existing lint/test commands
3. Create `lefthook.yml` at repo root with hooks:
   - **pre-commit**: fast checks only
     - Go: `go vet ./...` (fast)
     - Web: `pnpm lint` (fast)
   - **pre-push**: standard quality gate
     - Run `make test-quick` (or equivalent fast gate)
4. Create documentation in the config or a README section explaining:
   - How to install lefthook (`go install github.com/evilmartians/lefthook@latest` or `npm i lefthook -g`)
   - How to activate hooks: `lefthook install`
   - How to skip hooks when needed: `--no-verify` (with strong guidance to not abuse this)
5. Keep hooks fast — pre-commit should complete in <10s, pre-push in <30s
6. Do NOT install lefthook itself (it may not be available) — just create the config

## Quality Gate
Config-only change. Run: `make test-quick`

## When Done
Commit and push:
```bash
git add -A
git commit -m "feat: add Lefthook git hook configuration

Configures pre-commit (go vet, pnpm lint) and pre-push (make test-quick)
hooks for deterministic, shared hook orchestration.

Closes kapow-c0s.1

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>"
git push -u origin HEAD
```
Report completion to team lead.
</teammate-message>

## Kapow Monorepo Makefile — delegates to component Makefiles
## Usage: make <target>

# Load .env if present — makes vars available to shell commands in recipes
-include .env
export VITE_SUPABASE_URL VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY

.PHONY: api-build api-run api-generate api-test api-test-unit \
        api-lint api-migrate-up api-migrate-down api-clean schema-gen \
        web-install web-dev web-build web-lint web-clean web-test web-test-coverage web-e2e web-test-e2e web-test-e2e-critical web-screenshots web-storybook \
        web-schema-sync \
        lint audit hadolint \
        smoke-test smoke-test-with-server \
        api-image-build web-image-build test-image-build test-image-run \
        tools-build tools-test kapow-build kapow-test kapow-test-unit \
        dev dev-clean dev-prune dev-db-up dev-db-down \
        compose-up compose-down compose-logs \
        test-quick test-standard test-full test-fixture \
        fixture-sql fixture-json \
        build-full \
        spec-gen \
        demo \
        help

## api-build: Build the API server binary
api-build:
	$(MAKE) -C api build

## build-full: Build single binary with embedded web frontend (API + SPA)
build-full: web-build
	$(MAKE) -C api build-full

## api-run: Run the API server
api-run:
	$(MAKE) -C api run

## api-generate: Run GraphQL code generation
api-generate:
	$(MAKE) -C api generate

## api-test: Run all API tests
api-test:
	$(MAKE) -C api test

## api-test-unit: Run API unit tests
api-test-unit:
	$(MAKE) -C api test-unit

## api-lint: Run linter on the API
api-lint:
	$(MAKE) -C api lint

## api-migrate-up: Apply database migrations
api-migrate-up:
	$(MAKE) -C api migrate-up

## api-migrate-down: Roll back last database migration
api-migrate-down:
	$(MAKE) -C api migrate-down

## api-clean: Remove API build artifacts
api-clean:
	$(MAKE) -C api clean

## schema-gen: Generate common/schema/schema.sql from migration Up blocks
schema-gen:
	bash scripts/gen-schema.sh

## web-install: Install web frontend dependencies
web-install:
	$(MAKE) -C web install

## web-dev: Start Vite dev server (port 8080, proxies /graphql → :8081)
web-dev:
	$(MAKE) -C web dev

## web-build: Type-check and build web frontend for production
web-build:
	$(MAKE) -C web build

## web-lint: Run ESLint on web frontend
web-lint:
	$(MAKE) -C web lint

## web-clean: Remove web build artifacts and node_modules
web-clean:
	$(MAKE) -C web clean

## web-test: Run web unit tests
web-test:
	$(MAKE) -C web test

## web-test-coverage: Run web unit tests with coverage report
web-test-coverage:
	$(MAKE) -C web test-coverage

## web-e2e: Run Playwright E2E tests for web frontend (starts Vite dev server automatically)
web-e2e:
	$(MAKE) -C web e2e-test

## web-test-e2e: Run all Playwright E2E tests (alias with webServer auto-start)
web-test-e2e:
	$(MAKE) -C web e2e-test

## web-test-e2e-critical: Run only @critical tier E2E tests
web-test-e2e-critical:
	$(MAKE) -C web e2e-test-critical

## web-screenshots: Capture visual baseline screenshots across viewports
web-screenshots:
	$(MAKE) -C web screenshots

## web-storybook: Start Storybook dev server for UI components (port 6006)
web-storybook:
	$(MAKE) -C web storybook

## web-schema-sync: Regenerate web/schema.graphql and graphql-env.d.ts from API SDL files
web-schema-sync:
	cat api/graph/schema/schema.graphql \
	    api/graph/schema/auth.graphql \
	    api/graph/schema/data_sources.graphql \
	    api/graph/schema/echo.graphql \
	    api/graph/schema/field_names_gen.graphql \
	    api/graph/schema/institution.graphql \
	    api/graph/schema/preferences.graphql \
	    api/graph/schema/search.graphql \
	    api/graph/schema/search_fields.graphql \
	    api/graph/schema/similarity.graphql \
	    api/graph/schema/zip.graphql \
	    > web/schema.graphql
	cd web && pnpm exec gql-tada generate-output --tsconfig tsconfig.app.json

## lint: Run all linters (Go + web)
lint: api-lint web-lint

## audit: Run security audit on web dependencies (non-zero exit on high+ severity)
audit:
	cd web && pnpm audit --audit-level=high

## hadolint: Lint all Dockerfiles with hadolint (runs via Docker)
hadolint:
	@find . -name 'Dockerfile*' -not -path '*/node_modules/*' -not -path '*/dist/*' | sort | \
		while read -r f; do \
			echo "Linting $$f"; \
			docker run --rm -i hadolint/hadolint < "$$f" || exit 1; \
		done

## smoke-test: Run search smoke test against localhost:8081 (requires running API)
smoke-test:
	bash scripts/smoke-test.sh

## smoke-test-with-server: Build API, start it, run smoke test, tear down (requires DATABASE_URL)
smoke-test-with-server:
	bash scripts/smoke-test-with-server.sh

## api-image-build: Build the standalone API production Docker image
api-image-build:
	docker build -f api/Dockerfile \
		--build-arg VERSION=$$(git describe --tags --always --dirty 2>/dev/null || echo dev) \
		--build-arg BUILD_TIME=$$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) \
		-t kapow-api \
		api/

## web-image-build: Build the standalone web frontend Docker image (nginx + Vite build)
web-image-build:
	docker build \
		$$([ -n "$$VITE_SUPABASE_URL" ] && echo "--build-arg VITE_SUPABASE_URL=$$VITE_SUPABASE_URL") \
		$$([ -n "$$VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY" ] && echo "--build-arg VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=$$VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY") \
		-t kapow-web web/

## test-image-build: Build the all-in-one test Docker image (postgres+API+nginx)
test-image-build: hadolint
	docker build \
		$$([ -n "$$VITE_SUPABASE_URL" ] && echo "--build-arg VITE_SUPABASE_URL=$$VITE_SUPABASE_URL") \
		$$([ -n "$$VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY" ] && echo "--build-arg VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=$$VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY") \
		-t kapow-test .

## test-image-run: Run the test image, exposing the web UI on http://localhost:8080
test-image-run: test-image-build
	docker run --rm -p 8080:80 kapow-test

## demo: Build test image, run it, and launch the interactive Playwright demo
demo:
	bash scripts/demo.sh $(DEMO_ARGS)

## dev: Start full-stack dev environment (PostgreSQL + API + web) with hot reload
dev:
	bash scripts/dev.sh

## dev-clean: Remove this checkout's dev database volume (forces fresh fixture load on next `make dev`)
dev-clean:
	@HASH=$$(echo "$$PWD" | md5sum | cut -c1-8); \
	VOL="kapow-dev-pgdata-$$HASH"; \
	CONT="kapow-dev-$$HASH"; \
	docker rm -f "$$CONT" 2>/dev/null || true; \
	if docker volume inspect "$$VOL" >/dev/null 2>&1; then \
		docker volume rm "$$VOL"; \
		echo "Removed volume $$VOL"; \
	else \
		echo "Volume $$VOL does not exist"; \
	fi

## dev-db-up: Start standalone dev database (PostgreSQL+PostGIS on port 5433, full dataset)
dev-db-up:
	docker compose -f compose/db.yml up -d --wait
	@echo ""
	@echo "Dev database starting on port 5433. Use:"
	@echo "  DATABASE_URL=postgres://postgres:postgres@localhost:5433/kapow?sslmode=disable"

## dev-db-down: Stop and remove the standalone dev database container
dev-db-down:
	docker compose -f compose/db.yml down

## compose-up: Start 3-service production topology (db + api + web) on http://localhost:8080
compose-up:
	docker compose -f compose/compose.yml up -d --build --wait

## compose-down: Stop and remove compose services and volumes
compose-down:
	docker compose -f compose/compose.yml down -v

## compose-logs: Tail logs from all compose services
compose-logs:
	docker compose -f compose/compose.yml logs -f

## dev-prune: Remove kapow-dev volumes older than 30 days
dev-prune:
	@CUTOFF=$$(date -d '30 days ago' +%s 2>/dev/null || date -v-30d +%s); \
	for vol in $$(docker volume ls --filter label=kapow-dev=true -q 2>/dev/null); do \
		CREATED=$$(docker volume inspect "$$vol" --format '{{.CreatedAt}}' 2>/dev/null); \
		VOL_TS=$$(date -d "$$CREATED" +%s 2>/dev/null || date -j -f "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S" "$$CREATED" +%s 2>/dev/null || echo 0); \
		if [ "$$VOL_TS" -lt "$$CUTOFF" ]; then \
			echo "Removing old volume: $$vol (created $$CREATED)"; \
			docker volume rm "$$vol" 2>/dev/null || true; \
		fi; \
	done; \
	echo "Prune complete."

## kapow-build: Build the kapow data pipeline binary
kapow-build:
	cd tools/kapow && go build -o ../../bin/kapow .

## kapow-test: Run all kapow tool tests
kapow-test:
	cd tools/kapow && go test ./...

## kapow-test-unit: Run kapow unit tests only (no DB)
kapow-test-unit:
	cd tools/kapow && go test -short ./...

## tools-build: Build all tools (samplesql, kapow)
tools-build: kapow-build
	cd tools/samplesql && go build -o ../../bin/samplesql .

## tools-test: Run tests for all tools
tools-test: kapow-test
	cd tools/samplesql && go test ./...

## test-quick: Fast checks — Go unit tests, TS type-check + build, ESLint (~10-20s, no DB)
test-quick:
	MAKE=$(MAKE) bash scripts/parallel-run.sh api-test-unit web-build web-lint

## test-standard: Full local validation — quick + web unit tests + Go lint (~30-60s, no DB)
test-standard:
	$(MAKE) test-quick && MAKE=$(MAKE) bash scripts/parallel-run.sh web-test api-lint

## test-fixture: Validate fixture data loads cleanly against current schema (spins up temp Docker PG)
test-fixture:
	bash scripts/test-fixture.sh

## test-full: Complete validation — standard + integration, e2e, smoke, fixture (requires DB + running services)
test-full:
	$(MAKE) test-standard && MAKE=$(MAKE) bash scripts/parallel-run.sh api-test test-fixture tools-test && $(MAKE) web-test-e2e && $(MAKE) smoke-test

## fixture-sql: Generate complete.sql.xz from JSON fixture data (requires Docker)
fixture-sql:
	bash scripts/gen-fixture-sql.sh

## fixture-json: Re-extract JSON fixture data from a live database (requires DATABASE_URL)
fixture-json:
	cd tools/samplesql && go run . --format json --output-dir ../../common/src/main/resources/data/tables

## spec-gen: Generate reference documentation in docs/specs/generated/
spec-gen:
	bash scripts/gen-specs.sh

## fixture-sql: Generate complete.sql.xz from JSON fixture data (requires Docker)
fixture-sql:
	bash scripts/gen-fixture-sql.sh

## fixture-json: Re-extract JSON fixture data from a live database (requires DATABASE_URL)
fixture-json:
	cd tools/samplesql && go run . --format json --output-dir ../../common/src/main/resources/data/tables

## help: List all available targets
help:
	@ESSENTIAL="api-generate build-full dev help lint test-image-run test-full test-quick test-standard web-schema-sync"; \
	echo ""; \
	echo "Usage: make <target>"; \
	echo ""; \
	echo "Essential targets:"; \
	echo ""; \
	grep -hE '^## [a-z]' $(MAKEFILE_LIST) | sed 's/## //' | sort -t: -k1,1 | \
		while IFS=: read -r target desc; do \
			for e in $$ESSENTIAL; do \
				if [ "$$target" = "$$e" ]; then \
					printf "  %-24s %s\n" "$$target" "$$desc"; \
					break; \
				fi; \
			done; \
		done; \
	echo ""; \
	echo "All targets:"; \
	echo ""; \
	grep -hE '^## [a-z]' $(MAKEFILE_LIST) | sed 's/## //' | sort -t: -k1,1 | \
		while IFS=: read -r target desc; do \
			SKIP=false; \
			for e in $$ESSENTIAL; do \
				if [ "$$target" = "$$e" ]; then SKIP=true; break; fi; \
			done; \
			if [ "$$SKIP" = "false" ]; then \
				printf "  %-24s %s\n" "$$target" "$$desc"; \
			fi; \
		done

   141→Open **http://localhost:8080**.
   142→
   143→#### 5. Authentication with Supabase (optional)
   144→
   145→The GraphQL endpoint (`/graphql`) requires a valid JWT. In development you can
   146→use any HS256 secret via `JWT_SECRET`. For production (or to test real login
   147→flows), connect a Supabase project.

   187→   and add your Supabase callback URL as an authorized redirect URI in Google.
   188→3. **Apple**: Enable the Apple provider, upload your **Services ID**, **Team ID**,
   189→   **Key ID**, and **private key** (from
   190→   [Apple Developer → Certificates, Identifiers & Profiles](https://developer.apple.com/account/resources)).
   191→   Add the Supabase callback URL to your Apple Sign In configuration.
   192→
   193→Each provider's callback URL is shown in the Supabase dashboard when you enable
   194→it (typically `https://<project-ref>.supabase.co/auth/v1/callback`).
   195→
   196→##### How it works
   197→
   198→On the first authenticated request, the API automatically creates a personal
   199→team and membership for the user (using the JWT `sub` claim as the user ID).

   202→

   204→
   205→[![CI](https://github.com/ketang/kapow/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/ketang/kapow/actions/workflows/ci.yml)

   222→
   223→```
   224→kapow/
   225→├── api/          # Go API server (GraphQL, search engine, auth)
   226→├── web/          # React + TypeScript + Vite frontend
   227→├── common/       # Shared resources (generated schema, fixture data)
   228→├── docker/       # Container config (nginx, supervisord, entrypoint)
   229→├── scripts/      # Shell utilities (smoke tests, schema generation)
   230→├── tools/        # Developer utilities (see below)
   231→├── Dockerfile    # All-in-one multi-stage build
   232→└── Makefile      # Root — delegates to api/ and web/
   233→```
   234→
   235→### tools/
   236→
   237→Developer utilities that operate on the database or fixture data.
   238→
   239→| Tool | Description |
   240→|---|---|
   241→| `tools/kapow` | Data pipeline — fetches, caches, and imports data from government and third-party sources (NCES, etc.) into the Kapow PostgreSQL database. |
   242→| `tools/samplesql` | Deterministic institution sampler — connects to a live database, randomly selects N institutions (default 100, seed 42), extracts all dependent rows, and writes a self-contained SQL fixture file using `COPY` statements. Useful for generating small, reproducible test datasets. |
   243→
   244→**kapow usage:**
   245→
   246→```bash
   247→cd tools/kapow
   248→go run . --help              # show usage
   249→go run . import nces         # import NCES data
   250→go run . run-all             # fetch all + import all in dependency order
   251→```
   252→
   253→Make targets: `make kapow-build`, `make kapow-test`, `make kapow-test-unit`.
   254→
   255→**samplesql usage:**
   256→
   257→```bash
   258→cd tools/samplesql
   259→go run . --database-url "$DATABASE_URL" --limit 100 --seed 42 --output sample.sql
   260→```
   261→
   262→Flags: `--database-url` (or `DATABASE_URL` env var), `--limit` (number of institutions), `--seed` (deterministic random seed), `--output` (file path; defaults to stdout).
   263→
   264→---
   265→
   266→## Useful Commands
   267→
   268→### Development
   269→
   270→| Command | Description |
   271→|---|---|
   272→| `make dev` | Start full stack (DB + API + web) with hot reload |
   273→| `make dev-clean` | Remove dev DB volume (fresh fixture load on next run) |
   274→| `make dev-prune` | Remove kapow-dev volumes older than 30 days |
   275→
   276→### API
   277→
   278→| Command | Description |
   279→|---|---|
   280→| `make api-run` | Start the API server |
   281→| `make api-test` | Run all Go tests (with race detector) |
   282→| `make api-test-unit` | Unit tests only (no DB required) |
   283→| `make api-generate` | Regenerate GraphQL code after schema changes |
   284→| `make api-migrate-up` | Apply pending database migrations |
   285→| `make api-migrate-down` | Roll back the last migration |
   286→| `make api-lint` | Run go vet + golangci-lint (includes gosec) |
   287→| `make lint` | Run all linters (Go + web) |
   288→| `make audit` | Security audit on web dependencies |
   289→
   290→### Web
   291→
   292→| Command | Description |
   293→|---|---|
   294→| `make web-dev` | Start Vite dev server with HMR |
   295→| `make web-build` | TypeScript check + production build |
   296→| `make web-test` | Run Vitest unit tests |
   297→| `make web-lint` | ESLint (zero-warning policy) |
   298→| `make web-e2e` | Playwright end-to-end tests |
   299→| `make web-storybook` | Start Storybook on port 6006 |
   300→
   301→### Smoke Tests
   302→
   303→```bash
   304→# Against a running API
   305→bash scripts/smoke-test.sh
   306→
   307→# Builds the API, starts it, tests, and tears down automatically
   308→bash scripts/smoke-test-with-server.sh
   309→```
   310→
   311→---
   312→
   313→## Ports
   314→
   315→| Service | Port |
   316→|---|---|
   317→| Web dev server | 8080 |
   318→| API server | 8081 |
   319→| Dev PostgreSQL | 15432 |
   320→| Docker (all-in-one) | 8080 |
   321→| Storybook | 6006 |
   322→
   323→---
   324→
   325→## Environment Variables
   326→
   327→See `.env.example` for the full list. The essential ones:
   328→
   329→| Variable | Required | Description |
   330→|---|---|---|
   331→| `DATABASE_URL` | Yes | PostgreSQL connection string |
   332→| `JWT_SECRET` | Yes | Secret for HS256 token signing (or Supabase JWT secret) |
   333→| `JWT_PUBLIC_KEY` | No | RS256 PEM public key — overrides `JWT_SECRET` when set |
   334→| `JWT_ISSUER` | No | Expected `iss` claim (e.g. `https://<ref>.supabase.co/auth/v1`) |
   335→| `JWT_AUDIENCE` | No | Expected `aud` claim (e.g. `authenticated`) |
   336→| `GRAPHQL_PLAYGROUND` | No | Set `true` to enable GraphQL Playground at `/playground` |
   337→| `LOG_LEVEL` | No | `debug`, `info` (default), `warn`, `error` |
   338→| `LOG_FORMAT` | No | `json` (default) or `text` (dev) |
   339→| `CORS_ORIGINS` | No | Allowed origins, e.g. `http://localhost:8080` |
   340→

# Kapow — College Power Search

A college/university search platform with a Go API backend and React frontend.

## Quick Start with Docker (recommended)

The fastest way to try Kapow — a single Docker image bundles PostgreSQL, the API
server, and the web frontend with pre-loaded fixture data.

```bash
# Build the all-in-one image
make test-image-build

# Run it (exposes port 8080)
make test-image-run
```

Open **http://localhost:8080** in your browser. To run the interactive demo
against it, see [DEMO.md](DEMO.md).

The container includes:

- PostgreSQL 15 + PostGIS with pre-loaded college data
- Go API server on internal port 8081
- nginx reverse proxy serving the React frontend and proxying API requests

No configuration needed — the container handles everything on first boot,
including database initialization and fixture data loading.

To stop: press `Ctrl+C`.

---

## Devcontainer

See [.devcontainer/README.md](.devcontainer/README.md) for setup instructions
(GitHub Codespaces, VS Code, and CLI).

---

## Local Development Setup

### Prerequisites

- **Go** 1.23+
- **Node.js** 22+ with **pnpm** 9+ (`npm install -g pnpm`)
- **Docker** (for the dev database container)
- **psql** (PostgreSQL client) — `brew install libpq` on macOS, `apt install postgresql-client` on Linux

Optional:

- **golangci-lint** for linting
- **air** for standalone API hot-reload: `go install github.com/air-verse/air@latest` (`make dev` uses `go run` instead, no install needed)

### Quick start (recommended)

A single command starts everything — PostgreSQL in Docker, the Go API with hot
reload, and the Vite dev server:

```bash
make dev
```

This will:
- Start a PostgreSQL + PostGIS container on port **15432** (won't conflict with a host PostgreSQL)
- Load fixture data on first run (subsequent runs skip this step)
- Run database migrations
- Start the API with **air** (hot reload) on **:8081**
- Start the web dev server on **:8080** with HMR

Open **http://localhost:8080** and you're ready to go. Press **Ctrl+C** to stop
everything. The database volume persists between runs, so the next `make dev`
starts much faster.

```bash
make dev-clean    # remove this checkout's DB volume (forces fresh fixture load)
make dev-prune    # remove kapow-dev volumes older than 30 days
```

### Manual setup (alternative)

If you prefer to manage PostgreSQL yourself:

#### 1. Configure environment

```bash
cp .env.example .env
```

Edit `.env` and set at minimum:

```
DATABASE_URL=postgres://user:password@localhost:5432/kapow?sslmode=disable
JWT_SECRET=change-me-to-a-long-random-secret
```

#### 2. Set up the database

Create the database, then run migrations:

```bash
createdb kapow
make api-migrate-up
```

To load fixture data (from `common/src/main/resources/data/`):

```bash
xz -dc common/src/main/resources/data/complete.sql.xz | psql kapow
```

Smaller subsets are available for faster loading:

- `subset-VT.sql.xz` — Vermont only
- `subset-DE_MA_VT.sql.xz` — Delaware, Massachusetts, Vermont

#### 3. Start the API

```bash
make api-run
```

The API listens on **localhost:8081**. Verify with:

```bash
curl http://localhost:8081/private/healthcheck | jq .
```

#### 4. Start the web frontend

In a separate terminal:

```bash
make web-install   # first time only
make web-dev
```

The dev server runs on **localhost:8080** and proxies `/graphql` and `/api`
requests to the API at :8081.

Open **http://localhost:8080**.

#### 5. Authentication with Supabase (optional)

The GraphQL endpoint (`/graphql`) requires a valid JWT. In development you can
use any HS256 secret via `JWT_SECRET`. For production (or to test real login
flows), connect a Supabase project.

##### Create a Supabase project

1. Go to [supabase.com/dashboard](https://supabase.com/dashboard) and create a
   new project (or use an existing one).
2. Note your **Project URL** (`https://<project-ref>.supabase.co`) and
   **anon public** key from **Settings → API**.

##### Configure API environment variables

In `.env`, set the JWT vars so the Go API can validate Supabase-issued tokens:

```
JWT_SECRET=<your-supabase-jwt-secret>
JWT_ISSUER=https://<project-ref>.supabase.co/auth/v1
JWT_AUDIENCE=authenticated
```

The JWT Secret is on **Settings → API → JWT Secret**. Alternatively, paste the
**JWT Public Key** (RS256) into `JWT_PUBLIC_KEY` for asymmetric validation.

##### Configure frontend environment variables

The web frontend uses the Supabase JS SDK for login/signup. Add these to `.env`:

```
VITE_SUPABASE_URL=https://<project-ref>.supabase.co
VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=<your-anon-key>
```

The `VITE_` prefix is required so Vite exposes them to browser code.

##### Enable OAuth providers

To support social login (Google, Apple, etc.):

1. In the Supabase dashboard, go to **Authentication → Providers**.
2. **Google**: Enable the Google provider, paste your Google OAuth **Client ID**
   and **Client Secret** (from [Google Cloud Console → Credentials](https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/credentials)),
   and add your Supabase callback URL as an authorized redirect URI in Google.
3. **Apple**: Enable the Apple provider, upload your **Services ID**, **Team ID**,
   **Key ID**, and **private key** (from
   [Apple Developer → Certificates, Identifiers & Profiles](https://developer.apple.com/account/resources)).
   Add the Supabase callback URL to your Apple Sign In configuration.

Each provider's callback URL is shown in the Supabase dashboard when you enable
it (typically `https://<project-ref>.supabase.co/auth/v1/callback`).

##### How it works

On the first authenticated request, the API automatically creates a personal
team and membership for the user (using the JWT `sub` claim as the user ID).

---

## CI

[![CI](https://github.com/ketang/kapow/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/ketang/kapow/actions/workflows/ci.yml)

Every push to `main` and every pull request runs the **standard quality gate**
(`make test-standard`), which includes:

- Go unit tests (race detector, short mode)
- TypeScript type-check + production build
- ESLint (zero-warning policy)
- Vitest unit tests
- Go lint (`go vet` + `golangci-lint`)

No database is required — the gate runs in ~30-60 seconds. Failures block
merges.

---

## Project Structure

```
kapow/
├── api/          # Go API server (GraphQL, search engine, auth)
├── web/          # React + TypeScript + Vite frontend
├── common/       # Shared resources (generated schema, fixture data)
├── docker/       # Container config (nginx, supervisord, entrypoint)
├── scripts/      # Shell utilities (smoke tests, schema generation)
├── tools/        # Developer utilities (see below)
├── Dockerfile    # All-in-one multi-stage build
└── Makefile      # Root — delegates to api/ and web/
```

### tools/

Developer utilities that operate on the database or fixture data.

| Tool | Description |
|---|---|
| `tools/kapow` | Data pipeline — fetches, caches, and imports data from government and third-party sources (NCES, etc.) into the Kapow PostgreSQL database. |
| `tools/samplesql` | Deterministic institution sampler — connects to a live database, randomly selects N institutions (default 100, seed 42), extracts all dependent rows, and writes a self-contained SQL fixture file using `COPY` statements. Useful for generating small, reproducible test datasets. |

**kapow usage:**

```bash
cd tools/kapow
go run . --help              # show usage
go run . import nces         # import NCES data
go run . run-all             # fetch all + import all in dependency order
```

Make targets: `make kapow-build`, `make kapow-test`, `make kapow-test-unit`.

**samplesql usage:**

```bash
cd tools/samplesql
go run . --database-url "$DATABASE_URL" --limit 100 --seed 42 --output sample.sql
```

Flags: `--database-url` (or `DATABASE_URL` env var), `--limit` (number of institutions), `--seed` (deterministic random seed), `--output` (file path; defaults to stdout).

---

## Useful Commands

### Development

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `make dev` | Start full stack (DB + API + web) with hot reload |
| `make dev-clean` | Remove dev DB volume (fresh fixture load on next run) |
| `make dev-prune` | Remove kapow-dev volumes older than 30 days |

### API

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `make api-run` | Start the API server |
| `make api-test` | Run all Go tests (with race detector) |
| `make api-test-unit` | Unit tests only (no DB required) |
| `make api-generate` | Regenerate GraphQL code after schema changes |
| `make api-migrate-up` | Apply pending database migrations |
| `make api-migrate-down` | Roll back the last migration |
| `make api-lint` | Run go vet + golangci-lint (includes gosec) |
| `make lint` | Run all linters (Go + web) |
| `make audit` | Security audit on web dependencies |

### Web

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `make web-dev` | Start Vite dev server with HMR |
| `make web-build` | TypeScript check + production build |
| `make web-test` | Run Vitest unit tests |
| `make web-lint` | ESLint (zero-warning policy) |
| `make web-e2e` | Playwright end-to-end tests |
| `make web-storybook` | Start Storybook on port 6006 |

### Smoke Tests

```bash
# Against a running API
bash scripts/smoke-test.sh

# Builds the API, starts it, tests, and tears down automatically
bash scripts/smoke-test-with-server.sh
```

---

## Ports

| Service | Port |
|---|---|
| Web dev server | 8080 |
| API server | 8081 |
| Dev PostgreSQL | 15432 |
| Docker (all-in-one) | 8080 |
| Storybook | 6006 |

---

## Environment Variables

See `.env.example` for the full list. The essential ones:

| Variable | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `DATABASE_URL` | Yes | PostgreSQL connection string |
| `JWT_SECRET` | Yes | Secret for HS256 token signing (or Supabase JWT secret) |
| `JWT_PUBLIC_KEY` | No | RS256 PEM public key — overrides `JWT_SECRET` when set |
| `JWT_ISSUER` | No | Expected `iss` claim (e.g. `https://<ref>.supabase.co/auth/v1`) |
| `JWT_AUDIENCE` | No | Expected `aud` claim (e.g. `authenticated`) |
| `GRAPHQL_PLAYGROUND` | No | Set `true` to enable GraphQL Playground at `/playground` |
| `LOG_LEVEL` | No | `debug`, `info` (default), `warn`, `error` |
| `LOG_FORMAT` | No | `json` (default) or `text` (dev) |
| `CORS_ORIGINS` | No | Allowed origins, e.g. `http://localhost:8080` |

    13→```
    14→
    15→## Issue Title Guidelines
    16→
    17→Titles appear in `bd list`, `bd ready`, and terminal window titles where space
    18→is limited. The **first ~20 characters** must be meaningfully descriptive:
    19→
    20→| Bad | Good |
    21→|---|---|
    22→| `Add support for filtering by SAT score` | `SAT score filter for search` |
    23→| `Update the search page to show result counts` | `Search result count display` |
    24→| `Fix bug where geographic radius is ignored` | `Geo radius filter ignored` |
    25→| `Implement new card component for institutions` | `Institution card component` |
    26→
    27→- **Lead with the noun or area** ("Search …", "Auth …", "GeoField …"), not filler verbs
    28→- **Under 50 characters** — put detail in `--description`
    29→- Avoid prefixes like "Add support for", "Update the", "Implement new"
    30→
    31→## Creating Issues
    32→
    33→Use the correct issue type:
    34→- `epic` — a grouping container for related features/tasks. Not directly workable.
    35→- `feature` — delivers new user-visible or agent-visible capability
    36→- `task` — scaffold, chore, or infrastructure work (e.g., project init, CI setup)
    37→- `bug` — something broken that needs fixing
    38→
    39→### Creating Epics
    40→
    41→Epics must use `--waits-for-gate` so they are blocked until their children complete, and do not appear in `bd ready`:
    42→
    43→```bash
    44→bd create "Epic: Feature Area" -t epic -p 2 -l label1,label2 \
    45→  -d "Description of the epic" \
    46→  --waits-for-gate all-children
    47→```
    48→
    49→This makes the epic resolve automatically when all children are closed. Never leave an epic as a bare open issue with no gate — it will pollute `bd ready`.
    50→
    51→### Creating Child Issues Under Epics
    52→
    53→Use `--parent` to place issues under an epic, and `--waits-for` to make the epic wait for the child:
    54→
    55→```bash
    56→bd create "Implement feature X" -t feature -p 1 -l search,go \
    57→  --parent kapow-abc \
    58→  --waits-for kapow-abc \
    59→  -d "Description" \
    60→  --acceptance "Acceptance criteria"
    61→```
    62→
    63→The `--parent` flag establishes the hierarchy. The `--waits-for kapow-abc` flag tells the epic to wait for this child before it can close.
    64→
    65→### Dependencies Between Issues
    66→
    67→Use `bd dep add` to express ordering constraints between issues:
    68→
    69→```bash
    70→bd dep add kapow-child kapow-dependency   # kapow-child is blocked by kapow-dependency
    71→```
    72→
    73→Only add dependencies where there is a real technical ordering constraint (e.g., "the migration must exist before the resolver can query the new column"). Do not add dependencies for soft preferences or nice-to-have ordering.
    74→
    75→## Implementation Plans
    76→
    77→Claude Code saves plans to `~/.claude/plans/` with random names (e.g.,
    78→`replicated-drifting-meerkat.md`). These are local to the machine and lost
    79→when switching devices. **After a plan is approved, copy it into the repo**
    80→so it persists across machines and sessions.
    81→
    82→### Saving a plan to the repo
    83→
    84→After exiting plan mode, copy the plan file from `~/.claude/plans/` into
    85→`docs/plans/`, renaming it to something meaningful:
    86→
    87→```bash
    88→cp ~/.claude/plans/<random-name>.md docs/plans/<issue-id>-<short-name>.md
    89→```
    90→
    91→Examples:
    92→- `docs/plans/kapow-a1b-geo-search.md`
    93→- `docs/plans/auth-refactor.md` (if no issue exists yet)
    94→
    95→Use lowercase kebab-case. Prefer `<issue-id>-<short-name>` when an issue exists.
    96→
    97→### Referencing from issues
    98→
    99→Add a note to the beads issue pointing to the plan:
   100→
   101→```bash
   102→bd update <id> --notes="Plan: docs/plans/<issue-id>-<short-name>.md"
   103→```
   104→
   105→### Resuming work from a plan
   106→
   107→When starting a new session, check `docs/plans/` for in-progress plans.
   108→If a beads issue references a plan file, read it before starting work.
   109→
   110→### Lifecycle
   111→
   112→- Commit and push plan files alongside the code they describe
   113→- Update plan files as the approach evolves during implementation
   114→- Delete plan files after the work is fully merged and closed
   115→
   116→## Completing an Issue
   117→
   118→An issue is not complete until:
   119→1. All code changes pass the required quality gate (see below)
   120→2. Changes are committed
   121→3. The issue is closed with `bd close <id>`
   122→4. Changes are pushed to remote with `bd sync && git push`
   123→
   124→## Quality Gates (Mandatory)
   125→
   126→Every code change MUST pass the appropriate quality gate **before committing**.
   127→Do not skip gates, use `--no-verify`, or treat failures as acceptable.
   128→
   129→### Required gate by change type
   130→
   131→| Change type | Gate | Command |
   132→|---|---|---|
   133→| Go code only | Standard | `make test-standard` |
   134→| Web code only | Standard | `make test-standard` |
   135→| Go + Web | Standard | `make test-standard` |
   136→| GraphQL schema (`.graphql`) | Standard + regenerate | `make api-generate && make web-schema-sync && make test-standard` |
   137→| Database migration | Full | `make test-full` |
   138→| Fixture data | Fixture | `make test-fixture` |
   139→| Docs only | Quick | `make test-quick` |
   140→
   141→**Rules:**
   142→- The gate must exit with **zero errors and zero warnings**
   143→- Run the gate from the repo root, not a subdirectory
   144→- If your change spans multiple types, use the **strictest** gate
   145→- When in doubt, run `make test-standard` — it covers most cases
   146→
   147→### Responding to gate failures
   148→
   149→1. **Read the full error output** — identify the root cause, not just the first line
   150→2. **Fix the code, not the test** — unless the test itself is wrong
   151→3. **Re-run the complete gate command** — partial re-runs miss cascading failures
   152→4. **If the failure is in generated code**, regenerate first: `make api-generate` and/or `make web-schema-sync`
   153→5. **Never bypass**: no `--no-verify`, no `--force`, no skipping the gate
   154→
   155→## Contract Test Rules
   156→
   157→The search validation contract (`api/internal/search/contract.go`) defines a
   158→shared test matrix consumed by three entry-point test runners:
   159→
   160→- `api/internal/search/contract_test.go` — baseline (ground truth)
   161→- `api/internal/handler/contract_test.go` — CSV export adapter
   162→- `api/internal/mcp/contract_test.go` — MCP protocol adapter
   163→
   164→### When you MUST update `ContractCases()`
   165→
   166→- Adding, removing, or renaming a search field
   167→- Changing validation logic in `ValidateRequest`
   168→- Adding a new entry point that consumes `SearchRequest`
   169→- Changing auth requirements for search fields
   170→
   171→### Contract update workflow
   172→
   173→1. Edit `api/internal/search/contract.go` — add/modify cases
   174→2. Run `make api-test` — all three contract runners must pass
   175→3. If a new entry point is added, create a corresponding `contract_test.go` that
   176→   consumes `ContractCases()` with the same assertion pattern
   177→
   178→**All three contract test files must pass.** A change that passes in one runner
   179→but fails in another indicates contract drift — fix the adapter, not the contract.
   180→
   181→## Protected Surfaces
   182→
   183→These areas require extra verification beyond the standard gate. Changes to
   184→protected surfaces that skip the additional checks MUST be rejected.
   185→
   186→| Surface | Extra requirement | Why |
   187→|---|---|---|
   188→| Generated files (`graph/generated/`, `graph/model/`, `web/src/graphql-env.d.ts`) | Never edit manually; always regenerate with `make api-generate` / `make web-schema-sync` | Manual edits are silently overwritten on next generate |
   189→| Migration files (`api/migrations/`) | `make test-full` with live DB; update fixtures if schema changes | Runtime pgx type errors only surface with a live connection |
   190→| Search field registry (`api/internal/search/registry.go`) | Update contract cases; verify `TestBuildSQLAllFields` passes | Contract drift across entry points |
   191→| Auth middleware (`api/internal/middleware/auth.go`) | Integration tests with real JWT validation | Auth bugs are silent and security-critical |
   192→| GraphQL schema (`api/graph/schema/*.graphql`) | `make api-generate && make web-schema-sync` before any other checks | Stale generated code causes confusing type errors |
   193→| Fixture data (`common/src/main/resources/data/`) | `make test-fixture` | Fixture/schema mismatch breaks all integration tests |
   194→
   195→## Git Workflow
   196→
   197→When work is complete and verified (tests pass, build succeeds, lint is clean),
   198→merge directly into `main`. Only fall back to a pull request if there is an
   199→unresolvable problem merging from the command line (e.g. complex conflicts).
   200→
   201→### Commit messages and issue references
   202→
   203→When a commit includes changes to beads data (`.beads/` files), list all
   204→affected issue IDs in the commit message body. This makes it easy to trace
   205→which issues were created, updated, or closed in each push.
   206→
   207→Example:
   208→```
   209→Add geo search field and update filter UI
   210→
   211→Implements proximity-based search using PostGIS ST_DWithin.
   212→
   213→Issues: kapow-a1b, kapow-c2d, kapow-e3f
   214→```
   215→
   216→### Worktree workflow
   217→
   218→When working in a git worktree (e.g. started with `--worktree`), **always merge
   219→the branch back into `main` before ending the session.** Do not leave the
   220→worktree branch unmerged without asking.
   221→
   222→**Before merging a worktree branch:**
   223→- Preview conflicts first: `git merge --no-commit --no-ff <branch>` then `git merge --abort`
   224→- Rebase long-lived worktrees onto main before merging: `git rebase main` (from the worktree)
   225→
   226→**After merging a worktree that touches GraphQL schema files:**
   227→- Always regenerate: `make api-generate && make web-schema-sync`
   228→- `.gitattributes` marks generated files with `merge=ours` — conflicts auto-resolve to the current branch, but you must regenerate to get the correct content
   229→
   230→**Worktree cleanup — never for Claude-managed worktrees:**
   231→Claude Code manages worktree lifecycle automatically — both for `--worktree`
   232→sessions and for subagents using `isolation: "worktree"`. These worktrees are
   233→cleaned up on session/agent exit.
   234→
   235→**Rules:**
   236→- **Never** run `rm -rf` on any worktree directory — use `git worktree remove` instead, which properly updates git bookkeeping
   237→- **Never** run `git worktree remove` on a Claude-managed worktree (`.claude/worktrees/`) — let the session end naturally; manual removal mid-session breaks the working directory
   238→- `git worktree remove` is fine for non-Claude worktrees (e.g. user-created worktrees that need cleanup)
   239→
   240→## Landing the Plane (Session Completion)
   241→
   242→**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.
   243→
   244→**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**
   245→
   246→1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
   247→2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds
   248→3. **Update issue status** - Close finished work, update in-progress items
   249→4. **PUSH TO REMOTE** - This is MANDATORY:
   250→   ```bash
   251→   git pull --rebase
   252→   bd sync
   253→   git push
   254→   git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"
   255→   ```
   256→5. **Clean up** - Clear stashes, prune remote branches
   257→6. **Verify** - All changes committed AND pushed
   258→7. **Hand off** - Provide context for next session
   259→
   260→**CRITICAL RULES:**
   261→- Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds
   262→- NEVER stop before pushing - that leaves work stranded locally
   263→- NEVER say "ready to push when you are" - YOU must push
   264→- If push fails, resolve and retry until it succeeds
   265→
   266→
   267→<!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION -->
   268→## Issue Tracking with bd (beads)
   269→
   270→**IMPORTANT**: This project uses **bd (beads)** for ALL issue tracking. Do NOT use markdown TODOs, task lists, or other tracking methods.
   271→
   272→### Why bd?
   273→
   274→- Dependency-aware: Track blockers and relationships between issues
   275→- Git-friendly: Auto-syncs to JSONL for version control
   276→- Agent-optimized: JSON output, ready work detection, discovered-from links
   277→- Prevents duplicate tracking systems and confusion
   278→
   279→### Quick Start
   280→
   281→**Check for ready work:**
   282→
   283→```bash
   284→bd ready --json
   285→```
   286→
   287→**Create new issues:**
   288→
   289→```bash
   290→bd create "Issue title" --description="Detailed context" -t bug|feature|task -p 0-4 --json
   291→bd create "Issue title" --description="What this issue is about" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:bd-123 --json
   292→```
   293→
   294→**Claim and update:**
   295→
   296→```bash
   297→bd update bd-42 --status in_progress --json
   298→bd update bd-42 --priority 1 --json
   299→```
   300→
   301→**Complete work:**
   302→
   303→```bash
   304→bd close bd-42 --reason "Completed" --json
   305→```
   306→
   307→### Issue Types
   308→
   309→- `bug` - Something broken
   310→- `feature` - New functionality
   311→- `task` - Work item (tests, docs, refactoring)
   312→- `epic` - Large feature with subtasks
   313→- `chore` - Maintenance (dependencies, tooling)
   314→
   315→### Priorities
   316→
   317→- `0` - Critical (security, data loss, broken builds)
   318→- `1` - High (major features, important bugs)
   319→- `2` - Medium (default, nice-to-have)
   320→- `3` - Low (polish, optimization)
   321→- `4` - Backlog (future ideas)
   322→
   323→### Workflow for AI Agents
   324→
   325→1. **Check ready work**: `bd ready` shows unblocked issues
   326→2. **Claim your task**: `bd update <id> --status in_progress`
   327→3. **Work on it**: Implement, test, document
   328→4. **Discover new work?** Create linked issue:
   329→   - `bd create "Found bug" --description="Details about what was found" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:<parent-id>`
   330→5. **Complete**: `bd close <id> --reason "Done"`
   331→
   332→### Auto-Sync
   333→
   334→bd automatically syncs with git:
   335→
   336→- Exports to `.beads/issues.jsonl` after changes (5s debounce)
   337→- Imports from JSONL when newer (e.g., after `git pull`)
   338→- No manual export/import needed!
   339→
   340→### Important Rules
   341→
   342→- ✅ Use bd for ALL task tracking
   343→- ✅ Always use `--json` flag for programmatic use
   344→- ✅ Link discovered work with `discovered-from` dependencies
   345→- ✅ Check `bd ready` before asking "what should I work on?"
   346→- ❌ Do NOT create markdown TODO lists
   347→- ❌ Do NOT use external issue trackers
   348→- ❌ Do NOT duplicate tracking systems
   349→
   350→For more details, see README.md and docs/QUICKSTART.md.
   351→
   352→<!-- END BEADS INTEGRATION -->
   353→

# Agent Instructions

This project uses **bd** (beads) for issue tracking. Run `bd onboard` to get started.

## Quick Reference

```bash
bd ready              # Find available work
bd show <id>          # View issue details
bd update <id> --status in_progress  # Claim work
bd close <id>         # Complete work
bd sync               # Sync with git
```

## Issue Title Guidelines

Titles appear in `bd list`, `bd ready`, and terminal window titles where space
is limited. The **first ~20 characters** must be meaningfully descriptive:

| Bad | Good |
|---|---|
| `Add support for filtering by SAT score` | `SAT score filter for search` |
| `Update the search page to show result counts` | `Search result count display` |
| `Fix bug where geographic radius is ignored` | `Geo radius filter ignored` |
| `Implement new card component for institutions` | `Institution card component` |

- **Lead with the noun or area** ("Search …", "Auth …", "GeoField …"), not filler verbs
- **Under 50 characters** — put detail in `--description`
- Avoid prefixes like "Add support for", "Update the", "Implement new"

## Creating Issues

Use the correct issue type:
- `epic` — a grouping container for related features/tasks. Not directly workable.
- `feature` — delivers new user-visible or agent-visible capability
- `task` — scaffold, chore, or infrastructure work (e.g., project init, CI setup)
- `bug` — something broken that needs fixing

### Creating Epics

Epics must use `--waits-for-gate` so they are blocked until their children complete, and do not appear in `bd ready`:

```bash
bd create "Epic: Feature Area" -t epic -p 2 -l label1,label2 \
  -d "Description of the epic" \
  --waits-for-gate all-children
```

This makes the epic resolve automatically when all children are closed. Never leave an epic as a bare open issue with no gate — it will pollute `bd ready`.

### Creating Child Issues Under Epics

Use `--parent` to place issues under an epic, and `--waits-for` to make the epic wait for the child:

```bash
bd create "Implement feature X" -t feature -p 1 -l search,go \
  --parent kapow-abc \
  --waits-for kapow-abc \
  -d "Description" \
  --acceptance "Acceptance criteria"
```

The `--parent` flag establishes the hierarchy. The `--waits-for kapow-abc` flag tells the epic to wait for this child before it can close.

### Dependencies Between Issues

Use `bd dep add` to express ordering constraints between issues:

```bash
bd dep add kapow-child kapow-dependency   # kapow-child is blocked by kapow-dependency
```

Only add dependencies where there is a real technical ordering constraint (e.g., "the migration must exist before the resolver can query the new column"). Do not add dependencies for soft preferences or nice-to-have ordering.

## Implementation Plans

Claude Code saves plans to `~/.claude/plans/` with random names (e.g.,
`replicated-drifting-meerkat.md`). These are local to the machine and lost
when switching devices. **After a plan is approved, copy it into the repo**
so it persists across machines and sessions.

### Saving a plan to the repo

After exiting plan mode, copy the plan file from `~/.claude/plans/` into
`docs/plans/`, renaming it to something meaningful:

```bash
cp ~/.claude/plans/<random-name>.md docs/plans/<issue-id>-<short-name>.md
```

Examples:
- `docs/plans/kapow-a1b-geo-search.md`
- `docs/plans/auth-refactor.md` (if no issue exists yet)

Use lowercase kebab-case. Prefer `<issue-id>-<short-name>` when an issue exists.

### Referencing from issues

Add a note to the beads issue pointing to the plan:

```bash
bd update <id> --notes="Plan: docs/plans/<issue-id>-<short-name>.md"
```

### Resuming work from a plan

When starting a new session, check `docs/plans/` for in-progress plans.
If a beads issue references a plan file, read it before starting work.

### Lifecycle

- Commit and push plan files alongside the code they describe
- Update plan files as the approach evolves during implementation
- Delete plan files after the work is fully merged and closed

## Completing an Issue

An issue is not complete until:
1. All code changes pass the required quality gate (see below)
2. Changes are committed
3. The issue is closed with `bd close <id>`
4. Changes are pushed to remote with `bd sync && git push`

## Quality Gates (Mandatory)

Every code change MUST pass the appropriate quality gate **before committing**.
Do not skip gates, use `--no-verify`, or treat failures as acceptable.

### Required gate by change type

| Change type | Gate | Command |
|---|---|---|
| Go code only | Standard | `make test-standard` |
| Web code only | Standard | `make test-standard` |
| Go + Web | Standard | `make test-standard` |
| GraphQL schema (`.graphql`) | Standard + regenerate | `make api-generate && make web-schema-sync && make test-standard` |
| Database migration | Full | `make test-full` |
| Fixture data | Fixture | `make test-fixture` |
| Docs only | Quick | `make test-quick` |

**Rules:**
- The gate must exit with **zero errors and zero warnings**
- Run the gate from the repo root, not a subdirectory
- If your change spans multiple types, use the **strictest** gate
- When in doubt, run `make test-standard` — it covers most cases

### Responding to gate failures

1. **Read the full error output** — identify the root cause, not just the first line
2. **Fix the code, not the test** — unless the test itself is wrong
3. **Re-run the complete gate command** — partial re-runs miss cascading failures
4. **If the failure is in generated code**, regenerate first: `make api-generate` and/or `make web-schema-sync`
5. **Never bypass**: no `--no-verify`, no `--force`, no skipping the gate

## Contract Test Rules

The search validation contract (`api/internal/search/contract.go`) defines a
shared test matrix consumed by three entry-point test runners:

- `api/internal/search/contract_test.go` — baseline (ground truth)
- `api/internal/handler/contract_test.go` — CSV export adapter
- `api/internal/mcp/contract_test.go` — MCP protocol adapter

### When you MUST update `ContractCases()`

- Adding, removing, or renaming a search field
- Changing validation logic in `ValidateRequest`
- Adding a new entry point that consumes `SearchRequest`
- Changing auth requirements for search fields

### Contract update workflow

1. Edit `api/internal/search/contract.go` — add/modify cases
2. Run `make api-test` — all three contract runners must pass
3. If a new entry point is added, create a corresponding `contract_test.go` that
   consumes `ContractCases()` with the same assertion pattern

**All three contract test files must pass.** A change that passes in one runner
but fails in another indicates contract drift — fix the adapter, not the contract.

## Protected Surfaces

These areas require extra verification beyond the standard gate. Changes to
protected surfaces that skip the additional checks MUST be rejected.

| Surface | Extra requirement | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Generated files (`graph/generated/`, `graph/model/`, `web/src/graphql-env.d.ts`) | Never edit manually; always regenerate with `make api-generate` / `make web-schema-sync` | Manual edits are silently overwritten on next generate |
| Migration files (`api/migrations/`) | `make test-full` with live DB; update fixtures if schema changes | Runtime pgx type errors only surface with a live connection |
| Search field registry (`api/internal/search/registry.go`) | Update contract cases; verify `TestBuildSQLAllFields` passes | Contract drift across entry points |
| Auth middleware (`api/internal/middleware/auth.go`) | Integration tests with real JWT validation | Auth bugs are silent and security-critical |
| GraphQL schema (`api/graph/schema/*.graphql`) | `make api-generate && make web-schema-sync` before any other checks | Stale generated code causes confusing type errors |
| Fixture data (`common/src/main/resources/data/`) | `make test-fixture` | Fixture/schema mismatch breaks all integration tests |

## Git Workflow

When work is complete and verified (tests pass, build succeeds, lint is clean),
merge directly into `main`. Only fall back to a pull request if there is an
unresolvable problem merging from the command line (e.g. complex conflicts).

### Commit messages and issue references

When a commit includes changes to beads data (`.beads/` files), list all
affected issue IDs in the commit message body. This makes it easy to trace
which issues were created, updated, or closed in each push.

Example:
```
Add geo search field and update filter UI

Implements proximity-based search using PostGIS ST_DWithin.

Issues: kapow-a1b, kapow-c2d, kapow-e3f
```

### Worktree workflow

When working in a git worktree (e.g. started with `--worktree`), **always merge
the branch back into `main` before ending the session.** Do not leave the
worktree branch unmerged without asking.

**Before merging a worktree branch:**
- Preview conflicts first: `git merge --no-commit --no-ff <branch>` then `git merge --abort`
- Rebase long-lived worktrees onto main before merging: `git rebase main` (from the worktree)

**After merging a worktree that touches GraphQL schema files:**
- Always regenerate: `make api-generate && make web-schema-sync`
- `.gitattributes` marks generated files with `merge=ours` — conflicts auto-resolve to the current branch, but you must regenerate to get the correct content

**Worktree cleanup — never for Claude-managed worktrees:**
Claude Code manages worktree lifecycle automatically — both for `--worktree`
sessions and for subagents using `isolation: "worktree"`. These worktrees are
cleaned up on session/agent exit.

**Rules:**
- **Never** run `rm -rf` on any worktree directory — use `git worktree remove` instead, which properly updates git bookkeeping
- **Never** run `git worktree remove` on a Claude-managed worktree (`.claude/worktrees/`) — let the session end naturally; manual removal mid-session breaks the working directory
- `git worktree remove` is fine for non-Claude worktrees (e.g. user-created worktrees that need cleanup)

## Landing the Plane (Session Completion)

**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.

**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**

1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds
3. **Update issue status** - Close finished work, update in-progress items
4. **PUSH TO REMOTE** - This is MANDATORY:
   ```bash
   git pull --rebase
   bd sync
   git push
   git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"
   ```
5. **Clean up** - Clear stashes, prune remote branches
6. **Verify** - All changes committed AND pushed
7. **Hand off** - Provide context for next session

**CRITICAL RULES:**
- Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds
- NEVER stop before pushing - that leaves work stranded locally
- NEVER say "ready to push when you are" - YOU must push
- If push fails, resolve and retry until it succeeds


<!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION -->
## Issue Tracking with bd (beads)

**IMPORTANT**: This project uses **bd (beads)** for ALL issue tracking. Do NOT use markdown TODOs, task lists, or other tracking methods.

### Why bd?

- Dependency-aware: Track blockers and relationships between issues
- Git-friendly: Auto-syncs to JSONL for version control
- Agent-optimized: JSON output, ready work detection, discovered-from links
- Prevents duplicate tracking systems and confusion

### Quick Start

**Check for ready work:**

```bash
bd ready --json
```

**Create new issues:**

```bash
bd create "Issue title" --description="Detailed context" -t bug|feature|task -p 0-4 --json
bd create "Issue title" --description="What this issue is about" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:bd-123 --json
```

**Claim and update:**

```bash
bd update bd-42 --status in_progress --json
bd update bd-42 --priority 1 --json
```

**Complete work:**

```bash
bd close bd-42 --reason "Completed" --json
```

### Issue Types

- `bug` - Something broken
- `feature` - New functionality
- `task` - Work item (tests, docs, refactoring)
- `epic` - Large feature with subtasks
- `chore` - Maintenance (dependencies, tooling)

### Priorities

- `0` - Critical (security, data loss, broken builds)
- `1` - High (major features, important bugs)
- `2` - Medium (default, nice-to-have)
- `3` - Low (polish, optimization)
- `4` - Backlog (future ideas)

### Workflow for AI Agents

1. **Check ready work**: `bd ready` shows unblocked issues
2. **Claim your task**: `bd update <id> --status in_progress`
3. **Work on it**: Implement, test, document
4. **Discover new work?** Create linked issue:
   - `bd create "Found bug" --description="Details about what was found" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:<parent-id>`
5. **Complete**: `bd close <id> --reason "Done"`

### Auto-Sync

bd automatically syncs with git:

- Exports to `.beads/issues.jsonl` after changes (5s debounce)
- Imports from JSONL when newer (e.g., after `git pull`)
- No manual export/import needed!

### Important Rules

- ✅ Use bd for ALL task tracking
- ✅ Always use `--json` flag for programmatic use
- ✅ Link discovered work with `discovered-from` dependencies
- ✅ Check `bd ready` before asking "what should I work on?"
- ❌ Do NOT create markdown TODO lists
- ❌ Do NOT use external issue trackers
- ❌ Do NOT duplicate tracking systems

For more details, see README.md and docs/QUICKSTART.md.

# Plan: Lefthook Integration (kapow-c0s.1)

## Context

The repo lacks shared git hook orchestration. Developers and agents run ad-hoc
checks (or none) before committing/pushing, leading to inconsistent quality.
Lefthook provides fast, repo-local, declarative hook configuration that works
for both humans and automation.

## Changes

### 1. Create `lefthook.yml` at repo root

```yaml
pre-commit:
  parallel: true
  commands:
    go-vet:
      root: "api/"
      run: go vet ./...
    web-lint:
      root: "web/"
      run: pnpm lint

pre-push:
  commands:
    test-quick:
      run: make test-quick
```

- **pre-commit** runs `go vet` and `pnpm lint` in parallel (~5-10s)
- **pre-push** runs `make test-quick` which covers Go unit tests + TS build + ESLint (~10-20s)

### 2. Update `README.md` — add Git Hooks section

Add a section after the "CI" section documenting:
- What Lefthook is and why it's used
- How to install (`go install` or `npm i -g`)
- How to activate (`lefthook install`)
- How to skip when needed (`--no-verify`)

### 3. Update `.gitignore` if needed

Check if `.gitignore` already covers Lefthook's local override file (`lefthook-local.yml`).
If not, no action needed — it's optional and typically not gitignored.

## Files Modified

| File | Action |
|---|---|
| `lefthook.yml` | Create — hook configuration |
| `README.md` | Edit — add Git Hooks section |

## Verification

```bash
make test-quick   # config-only change, quick gate suffices
```

# Kapow — College Power Search

A college/university search platform with a Go API backend and React frontend.

## Quick Start with Docker (recommended)

The fastest way to try Kapow — a single Docker image bundles PostgreSQL, the API
server, and the web frontend with pre-loaded fixture data.

```bash
# Build the all-in-one image
make test-image-build

# Run it (exposes port 8080)
make test-image-run
```

Open **http://localhost:8080** in your browser. To run the interactive demo
against it, see [DEMO.md](DEMO.md).

The container includes:

- PostgreSQL 15 + PostGIS with pre-loaded college data
- Go API server on internal port 8081
- nginx reverse proxy serving the React frontend and proxying API requests

No configuration needed — the container handles everything on first boot,
including database initialization and fixture data loading.

To stop: press `Ctrl+C`.

---

## Devcontainer

See [.devcontainer/README.md](.devcontainer/README.md) for setup instructions
(GitHub Codespaces, VS Code, and CLI).

---

## Local Development Setup

### Prerequisites

- **Go** 1.23+
- **Node.js** 22+ with **pnpm** 9+ (`npm install -g pnpm`)
- **Docker** (for the dev database container)
- **psql** (PostgreSQL client) — `brew install libpq` on macOS, `apt install postgresql-client` on Linux

Optional:

- **golangci-lint** for linting
- **air** for standalone API hot-reload: `go install github.com/air-verse/air@latest` (`make dev` uses `go run` instead, no install needed)

### Quick start (recommended)

A single command starts everything — PostgreSQL in Docker, the Go API with hot
reload, and the Vite dev server:

```bash
make dev
```

This will:
- Start a PostgreSQL + PostGIS container on port **15432** (won't conflict with a host PostgreSQL)
- Load fixture data on first run (subsequent runs skip this step)
- Run database migrations
- Start the API with **air** (hot reload) on **:8081**
- Start the web dev server on **:8080** with HMR

Open **http://localhost:8080** and you're ready to go. Press **Ctrl+C** to stop
everything. The database volume persists between runs, so the next `make dev`
starts much faster.

```bash
make dev-clean    # remove this checkout's DB volume (forces fresh fixture load)
make dev-prune    # remove kapow-dev volumes older than 30 days
```

### Manual setup (alternative)

If you prefer to manage PostgreSQL yourself:

#### 1. Configure environment

```bash
cp .env.example .env
```

Edit `.env` and set at minimum:

```
DATABASE_URL=postgres://user:password@localhost:5432/kapow?sslmode=disable
JWT_SECRET=change-me-to-a-long-random-secret
```

#### 2. Set up the database

Create the database, then run migrations:

```bash
createdb kapow
make api-migrate-up
```

To load fixture data (from `common/src/main/resources/data/`):

```bash
xz -dc common/src/main/resources/data/complete.sql.xz | psql kapow
```

Smaller subsets are available for faster loading:

- `subset-VT.sql.xz` — Vermont only
- `subset-DE_MA_VT.sql.xz` — Delaware, Massachusetts, Vermont

#### 3. Start the API

```bash
make api-run
```

The API listens on **localhost:8081**. Verify with:

```bash
curl http://localhost:8081/private/healthcheck | jq .
```

#### 4. Start the web frontend

In a separate terminal:

```bash
make web-install   # first time only
make web-dev
```

The dev server runs on **localhost:8080** and proxies `/graphql` and `/api`
requests to the API at :8081.

Open **http://localhost:8080**.

#### 5. Authentication with Supabase (optional)

The GraphQL endpoint (`/graphql`) requires a valid JWT. In development you can
use any HS256 secret via `JWT_SECRET`. For production (or to test real login
flows), connect a Supabase project.

##### Create a Supabase project

1. Go to [supabase.com/dashboard](https://supabase.com/dashboard) and create a
   new project (or use an existing one).
2. Note your **Project URL** (`https://<project-ref>.supabase.co`) and
   **anon public** key from **Settings → API**.

##### Configure API environment variables

In `.env`, set the JWT vars so the Go API can validate Supabase-issued tokens:

```
JWT_SECRET=<your-supabase-jwt-secret>
JWT_ISSUER=https://<project-ref>.supabase.co/auth/v1
JWT_AUDIENCE=authenticated
```

The JWT Secret is on **Settings → API → JWT Secret**. Alternatively, paste the
**JWT Public Key** (RS256) into `JWT_PUBLIC_KEY` for asymmetric validation.

##### Configure frontend environment variables

The web frontend uses the Supabase JS SDK for login/signup. Add these to `.env`:

```
VITE_SUPABASE_URL=https://<project-ref>.supabase.co
VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=<your-anon-key>
```

The `VITE_` prefix is required so Vite exposes them to browser code.

##### Enable OAuth providers

To support social login (Google, Apple, etc.):

1. In the Supabase dashboard, go to **Authentication → Providers**.
2. **Google**: Enable the Google provider, paste your Google OAuth **Client ID**
   and **Client Secret** (from [Google Cloud Console → Credentials](https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/credentials)),
   and add your Supabase callback URL as an authorized redirect URI in Google.
3. **Apple**: Enable the Apple provider, upload your **Services ID**, **Team ID**,
   **Key ID**, and **private key** (from
   [Apple Developer → Certificates, Identifiers & Profiles](https://developer.apple.com/account/resources)).
   Add the Supabase callback URL to your Apple Sign In configuration.

Each provider's callback URL is shown in the Supabase dashboard when you enable
it (typically `https://<project-ref>.supabase.co/auth/v1/callback`).

##### How it works

On the first authenticated request, the API automatically creates a personal
team and membership for the user (using the JWT `sub` claim as the user ID).

---

## CI

[![CI](https://github.com/ketang/kapow/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/ketang/kapow/actions/workflows/ci.yml)

Every push to `main` and every pull request runs the **standard quality gate**
(`make test-standard`), which includes:

- Go unit tests (race detector, short mode)
- TypeScript type-check + production build
- ESLint (zero-warning policy)
- Vitest unit tests
- Go lint (`go vet` + `golangci-lint`)

No database is required — the gate runs in ~30-60 seconds. Failures block
merges.

---

## Project Structure

```
kapow/
├── api/          # Go API server (GraphQL, search engine, auth)
├── web/          # React + TypeScript + Vite frontend
├── common/       # Shared resources (generated schema, fixture data)
├── docker/       # Container config (nginx, supervisord, entrypoint)
├── scripts/      # Shell utilities (smoke tests, schema generation)
├── tools/        # Developer utilities (see below)
├── Dockerfile    # All-in-one multi-stage build
└── Makefile      # Root — delegates to api/ and web/
```

### tools/

Developer utilities that operate on the database or fixture data.

| Tool | Description |
|---|---|
| `tools/kapow` | Data pipeline — fetches, caches, and imports data from government and third-party sources (NCES, etc.) into the Kapow PostgreSQL database. |
| `tools/samplesql` | Deterministic institution sampler — connects to a live database, randomly selects N institutions (default 100, seed 42), extracts all dependent rows, and writes a self-contained SQL fixture file using `COPY` statements. Useful for generating small, reproducible test datasets. |

**kapow usage:**

```bash
cd tools/kapow
go run . --help              # show usage
go run . import nces         # import NCES data
go run . run-all             # fetch all + import all in dependency order
```

Make targets: `make kapow-build`, `make kapow-test`, `make kapow-test-unit`.

**samplesql usage:**

```bash
cd tools/samplesql
go run . --database-url "$DATABASE_URL" --limit 100 --seed 42 --output sample.sql
```

Flags: `--database-url` (or `DATABASE_URL` env var), `--limit` (number of institutions), `--seed` (deterministic random seed), `--output` (file path; defaults to stdout).

---

## Useful Commands

### Development

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `make dev` | Start full stack (DB + API + web) with hot reload |
| `make dev-clean` | Remove dev DB volume (fresh fixture load on next run) |
| `make dev-prune` | Remove kapow-dev volumes older than 30 days |

### API

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `make api-run` | Start the API server |
| `make api-test` | Run all Go tests (with race detector) |
| `make api-test-unit` | Unit tests only (no DB required) |
| `make api-generate` | Regenerate GraphQL code after schema changes |
| `make api-migrate-up` | Apply pending database migrations |
| `make api-migrate-down` | Roll back the last migration |
| `make api-lint` | Run go vet + golangci-lint (includes gosec) |
| `make lint` | Run all linters (Go + web) |
| `make audit` | Security audit on web dependencies |

### Web

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `make web-dev` | Start Vite dev server with HMR |
| `make web-build` | TypeScript check + production build |
| `make web-test` | Run Vitest unit tests |
| `make web-lint` | ESLint (zero-warning policy) |
| `make web-e2e` | Playwright end-to-end tests |
| `make web-storybook` | Start Storybook on port 6006 |

### Smoke Tests

```bash
# Against a running API
bash scripts/smoke-test.sh

# Builds the API, starts it, tests, and tears down automatically
bash scripts/smoke-test-with-server.sh
```

---

## Ports

| Service | Port |
|---|---|
| Web dev server | 8080 |
| API server | 8081 |
| Dev PostgreSQL | 15432 |
| Docker (all-in-one) | 8080 |
| Storybook | 6006 |

---

## Environment Variables

See `.env.example` for the full list. The essential ones:

| Variable | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `DATABASE_URL` | Yes | PostgreSQL connection string |
| `JWT_SECRET` | Yes | Secret for HS256 token signing (or Supabase JWT secret) |
| `JWT_PUBLIC_KEY` | No | RS256 PEM public key — overrides `JWT_SECRET` when set |
| `JWT_ISSUER` | No | Expected `iss` claim (e.g. `https://<ref>.supabase.co/auth/v1`) |
| `JWT_AUDIENCE` | No | Expected `aud` claim (e.g. `authenticated`) |
| `GRAPHQL_PLAYGROUND` | No | Set `true` to enable GraphQL Playground at `/playground` |
| `LOG_LEVEL` | No | `debug`, `info` (default), `warn`, `error` |
| `LOG_FORMAT` | No | `json` (default) or `text` (dev) |
| `CORS_ORIGINS` | No | Allowed origins, e.g. `http://localhost:8080` |
